title: Fun with Eva and Jung
wikitext:
[]: Fun with Eva and Jung             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Nov 12, 2007 10:30 pm I want to make this VERY clear that I am not going to form any kind of Jungian framework to understand every fictional element in NGE ala JFaulkner from ANF. But I have been reading Jung and it is impossible not to draw comparisons with him and NGE's themes. While I think it's pointless to try and use him to understand the sci-fi elements, the psychology is another matter. I just wanted to post this little part I found quite interesting: "All those who do not wish to deprive themselves of the great treasures that lie buried in the collective psyche will strive by every means possible to maintain their newly won connection with the primal source of life. Identification would seem to be the shortest road to this, for the dissolution of the persona in the collective psyche positively invites one to wed oneself with the abyss and blot out all memory in its embrace. This piece of mysticism is innate in all better men as the 'longing for the mother,' the nostalgia for the source from which we came." It's a little wordy, but really simple if you understand Jung's terminology. I can explain it in simpler terms if anyone doesn't understand the above. Anyway, I may post some more interesting connections as I find them. This isn't in discussion because I don't want people to think I'm going overboard with this. I'm just making parallels, not using Jung to explain NGE (though it is helpful in getting a deeper understanding of the characters' psyches).

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Wed Nov 14, 2007 7:40 am Ahoy there! Yeah, when you're reading some of his essays certain paragraphs will jump out at you as having resonance with some aspect of NGE. I stuck a bookmark on some page in "man without a soul" I think it was of his because it described Rei to a tee. Someone many moons ago on this forum said Stanislav Grof's books were a good read for NGE fans, I read a couple of his books after that but I don't see the connection as much as s/he implied there was...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Nov 14, 2007 8:15 am Only 150 or so pages in and I must say it's been quite enlightening. Not only does Jung seem to relate strongly to Eva, but to myself personally, and life in general. Even with some of his ideas which I immediately question he seems to do a good job of convincing me otherwise as I read on.

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Thu Nov 15, 2007 10:43 am 150 pages into what? What's the name of the book? Hope you don't mind if I put this here, seeing as you're talking about books and eva - I started reading this book called ancient philosophy and came across this paragraph, I'm not going to type it all out so here's a quick scan - Funny, ain't it? Embryo gods, amniotic fluids and umbilical cables!

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Nov 15, 2007 10:53 am Hehe, good find. The book is the Portable Jung - which is a collection of his work. It got good reviews and seemed like a good place to start.

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Tue Dec 04, 2007 1:14 pm I don't know if you've gotten to this yet but Jung's process of individuation seems to parallel the ultimate fate of the children in EoE, and the mythological hero's journey in general.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Dec 04, 2007 1:26 pm I passed that a while back, though I get the sense they cut that part a bit short for this book.

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Tue Dec 04, 2007 1:29 pm That's one of his most interesting aspects for me at least, you should check out, man and his symbols, which devotes an entire chapter to it, as well as one to a summery of jung by jung. And also Jung on active imagination which talks about how one can undergo this process, and what part active imagination plays in it.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Wed Dec 05, 2007 7:06 am Sweet find, Zugs. Embryos, umbilical cables and amniotic fluid, hah! I do find the idea(Assuming that Jung is relevant in Eva) that Homo Sapiens remains embryonic after being the product of a few billion years is a little bit screwy. Either that or the FAR are really, really Ancient.

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Wed Dec 05, 2007 11:25 am another Eva thing that reminds me of Jung is the secene in weaving a story where Misato, Asuka, and Rei all ask Shinji if they want to become one with him. That seems to me to have something to do with Jung's idea of anima, as if Shinji had his pick between them as not only a relationship, but also a part of his personality, which I think ends up coming through in the end when he decides to go on living with Asuka.

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Thu Dec 06, 2007 3:59 pm This is from one of those books on Jung again, I'm looking for one specific image that someone on animeboards brought up but came across this instead - EVA 02! Haven't bothered to read the book as it reads like pie in the sky stuff, but the proceeding page talks about the anima being associated with common fourfold divisions like Eve (lolwut), and it goes on for a paragraph that the anima is the mediator between ego and "the Self" - make any sense to you, Jimbo?

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Thu Dec 06, 2007 8:22 pm For a second there I thought it said "Brikhauser."

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Fri Dec 07, 2007 5:21 pm Zugzwang wrote: Haven't bothered to read the book as it reads like pie in the sky stuff, but the proceeding page talks about the anima being associated with common fourfold divisions like Eve (lolwut), and it goes on for a paragraph that the anima is the mediator between ego and "the Self" - make any sense to you, Jimbo?

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Sat Dec 08, 2007 1:18 pm Squirrel Ninja wrote: Zugzwang wrote: Haven't bothered to read the book as it reads like pie in the sky stuff, but the proceeding page talks about the anima being associated with common fourfold divisions like Eve (lolwut), and it goes on for a paragraph that the anima is the mediator between ego and "the Self" - make any sense to you, Jimbo?
